student_input: Oh wait, I see the problem! My function calls `factorial(n)` with the same `n`, so it will just keep calling itself forever. I need a base case to stop it, like when n equals 0 or 1, and then for the recursive case I should call `factorial(n-1)` to make progress toward that base case.
tutor_response: Great insight! Setting a base case ensures your recursive function stops calling itself infinitely. For example, when n equals 0 or 1, you can return 1 because 0! and 1! are both 1. Then, for other values of n, your function should call `factorial(n-1)` to move closer to the base case. How would you implement this?

Great recognition of the need for a base case! Here’s a reminder: A base case stops the recursion, typically when n equals 0 or 1. For the recursive case, you multiply n by the result of `factorial(n-1)`. Make sure your function checks for the base case first before making the recursive call. Give it a try!
